data.title: The New Defensive Playbook: Defense Spending As A Long-Term Reality
data.description:
By Samuel Rines and Jonathan Flynn

From Headline Risk to Structural Reality

Geopolitical risk has moved from a background concern to a central portfolio variable. Institutional surveys increasingly rank it as one of the most difficult risks to model, surpassing even inflation in some cases.

Yet today’s environment is not simply about short-term conflict headlines. It reflects a deeper transformation: a sustained global rearmament cycle, driven by modernization, technological competition and shifting alliances.

For investors, the key question is not whether tensions will fluctuate. It is whether defense spending, particularly in Europe and Asia, has entered a durable growth phase. We believe it has.

A Multi-Decade Rearmament Cycle Is Underway

Recent events have highlighted the effectiveness of modern Western defense systems, particularly interceptors, radar, drones and electronic detection. At the same time, they have exposed the rapid depletion of existing inventories.

That combination matters.

Defense contractors are not just working through existing backlogs. They are moving into what could be described as “far logs”, orders extending well into the next decade. Replenishment alone can stretch delivery timelines from three to five years out to 2030 and beyond.

This dynamic is not dependent on perpetual conflict. Even in a calmer geopolitical environment, countries are rebuilding inventories, upgrading systems and hardening domestic defense capabilities. In many cases, the modernization push is structural and bipartisan.

Defense is increasingly resembling a subscription model for governments, steady, recurring and essential.

The Pivot to Modern Warfare

One of the most important shifts investors may be underappreciating is the nature of modern defense.

This is no longer just about aircraft carriers or traditional heavy hardware. Increasingly, it is about:

  • Drone systems and counter-drone technology
  • Radar and early detection systems
  • Small-munition interception
  • AI-driven battlefield analytics
  • Advanced communications and electronic components

In Europe, where large-cap technology ecosystems are limited, defense companies are often at the forefront of advanced engineering and R&D. In Asia, particularly Japan and South Korea, electronic components and precision interception technologies are global leaders.

Defense innovation has historically spilled into civilian technology. The Cold War catalyzed many of the foundational technologies that underpin today’s digital economy. It would be premature to assume the current cycle will not generate similar second-order innovations.

For investors, this means the “industrial” label often attached to these companies understates their technological depth.

Valuations and the “Overbought” Question

After strong performance, investors naturally ask: Has the opportunity already played out?

It is important to distinguish between price momentum and structural earnings visibility.

Defense firms in Europe and Asia are benefiting from:

  • Expanding order books
  • Government commitments to multi-year spending increases
  • Inventory replenishment cycles
  • Technological upgrading

These factors provide forward revenue clarity that is uncommon in many equity sectors.

Even if geopolitical tensions ease in the near term, spending trajectories are unlikely to reverse. Defense budgets, once increased, rarely retreat meaningfully, especially amid rising global fragmentation and great-power competition.

Why Broad International Exposure May Miss the Theme

Many investors assume they already own this opportunity through broad international or emerging markets ETFs. In reality, that exposure is minimal.

Traditional international indexes are often dominated by financials and value-oriented sectors. Defense allocations are typically small, and overlap with dedicated defense strategies is limited.

This creates two important portfolio benefits:

  1. Low overlap and differentiation from traditional international holdings.
  2. Lower correlation relative to U.S. growth-heavy benchmarks.

For investors seeking diversification away from concentrated U.S. technology exposure, European and Asian defense equities represent a distinct return stream.

Portfolio Application: Targeted Access to Europe, Asia and Global Defense

WisdomTree provides targeted exposure through:

  • The WisdomTree Europe Defense Fund (WDEF)
  • The WisdomTree Asia Defense Fund (WDAF)
  • The WisdomTree Global Defense Fund (WDGF)

These strategies offer access to companies that are often difficult to purchase directly on U.S. exchanges and may lack liquid American Depositary Receipts.

These funds focus on firms positioned to benefit from modernization, technological advancement and expanding global defense budgets. Sector exposure skews toward industrials, but with meaningful embedded technology components.

For investors looking to diversify U.S. equity exposure, particularly concentrated positions in mega-cap growth, these strategies can serve as complementary allocations with differentiated drivers.

Conclusion: A Structural Shift, not a Tactical Trade

Europe and key Asian allies are building independent and technologically advanced defense capabilities. This buildout appears durable, bipartisan and multi-year.

For investors, the takeaway is clear: defense spending in Europe, Asia and globally has moved beyond a headline-driven trade. It is a structural allocation story, supported by modernization, replenishment and innovation.

In a world where geopolitical risk is harder to model than inflation, targeted exposure to global defense may serve not only as an opportunity, but as a portfolio stabilizer.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including potential loss of principal. Foreign investing involves specific risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

WDEF: A Fund focusing its investments on certain sectors and/or regions increases its vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility.

WDAF: Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility.

Because the Fund invests primarily in the securities of companies in Asia, the Fund’s performance is expected to be closely tied to social, political, and economic conditions within Asia and to be more volatile than the performance of more geographically diversified funds. Many countries in the region have historically faced political uncertainty, corruption, military intervention, and social unrest. To the extent that such events continue in the future, they can be expected to have an unpredictable effect on economic and securities market conditions in the region and may impact the ability of the Fund to buy, sell or otherwise transfer securities and cause the Fund to decline in value.

Investments in non-U.S. securities involve political, regulatory, and economic risks that may not be present in investments in U.S. securities.

WDGF: To the extent the Fund invests a significant portion of its assets in securities of companies of a single country or region, it is more likely to be impacted by events or conditions affecting that country or region. Investments in non-U.S. securities involve political, regulatory, and economic risks that may not be present in investments in U.S. securities.

Investments in non-U.S. securities may be subject to risk of loss due to foreign currency fluctuations, political or economic instability, or geographic events that adversely impact issuers of foreign securities. Investments in non-U.S. securities also may be subject to withholding or other taxes and may be subject to additional trading, settlement, custodial, and operational risks. These and other factors can make investments in the Fund more volatile and potentially less liquid than other types of investments. These risks may be heightened to the extent the Fund invests in companies domiciled in or otherwise tied to developing or emerging market countries.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy”.

Jonathan Flynn, Director of Market Strategy

data.title: Why February's CPI Report Matters More Than Ever Amid War-Driven Inflation
data.description:
A month after the Bureau of Labor Statistics favorably low January Consumer Price Index figures, it will release February’s report on Wednesday, March 11. The upcoming report matters more than ever for two main reasons. First, energy inflation risks are in play in March, so investors would need to wait another month before the official CPI figures. Secondly, the January CPI of 0.2% is 30 bps lower than the Producer Price Index of 0.5%.

What should investors and consumers expect from the February CPI report?

January CPI Increased 0.2%

The BLS reported a CPI 0.2% increase. As usual, the index for shelter (+0.2%) accounted for most of the all items monthly increase. The index for energy (-1.5%) helped offset the headline figure. From a topline view, services topped the increase that month. The index for food (+0.2%) is still above the Fed’s annual 2.0% inflation target rate (2.9%).

Looking at the sub-items, the index for transportation services led the list. This includes gas, insurance, maintenance, and transit costs. Stock markets continued to price in weaker demand for ride-hailing in the last few months. Shares in both Uber and Lyft fell in 2026. Flying-vehicle firm Joby Aviation (JOBY), which is one of Uber’s Seeking Alpha Peers, fell by over 25% YTD.

Uber is ranked first in the industry and 232nd in the industrial sector. This is an unfair ranking for the firm, since aerospace and defense is 24.2% of the market weight in this measure.

Amid the ongoing war economy, RTX (RTX), Boeing (BA), and General Electric (GE) are among the top industrial stocks. Uber is in eighth place with a 1.99% market weight.

Index for Medicare Care Services

The index for medical care services increased by 3.9% in the 12-months in the January report. Investors should expect this to increase again in February. The Federal Reserve Bank of New York recognized that higher health insurance costs dampened wage growth. GLP-1 obesity drugs skew the higher healthcare spending, helping Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO). Still, Fed Chair Jerome Powell counted on Americans benefiting from wage growth to counter the impact inflation has on eroding the consumer’s disposable income.

In the drug manufacturing sector, readers may also consider Pfizer (PFE), AbbVie (ABBV), and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY):

In the Quant scores above, Pfizer and Novo both score an A- or higher on valuation. Lilly has the highest industry rank of 20/177.

Warning on Energy Services Inflation

The index for energy services might increase again in February. It rose by 0.2% in January and by 7.2% in the year. To prevent consumers from blaming AI data centers, the government pressured tech companies to pay for AI data center power costs.

Companies in the energy services sector to consider include SLB (SLB), Baker Hughes (BKR), and Halliburton (HAL). Shares are up by at least 20% in 2026:

Investors would pay a premium, since the valuation grade is a D- or lower. Readers might want to wait for the momentum grade to weaken.

In the infrastructure and services sector, consider Williams Companies (WMB), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and Kinder Morgan (KMI).

Above: EPD stock has the best valuation grade of “B.” However, it scores the worst on EPS revisions and on growth. All three firms have attractive profitability grades.

February Price Decrease

Expect the CPI for used cars and trucks to decline like it did in January. Contrast that trend with new vehicle prices that increased. After the Iran invasion, shares of Ford Motor (F) and General Motors (GM) fell meaningfully. F stock lost 14% in the last week, while GM stock fell by around 6%.

The military conflict in Iran might lead to an increase in gas prices by 15% to 20%. Oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz hurt global oil supplies. Additionally, the gas price increase includes the annual switch from winter to summer gas. Summer gas costs more to produce, so consumers should brace for an uptrend in the price of gasoline. That would hurt the demand for new and used vehicles.

Note On January’s Producer Price Index

The BLS reported the Producer Price Index for final demand rose by 0.5%. The index for final demand services increased 0.8%. The sharp rise is a result of margins for final demand trade services (+2.5%). Look out for the impact of inflation on wholesalers and retailers showing up for consumers in future CPI reports.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets are forward-looking. So, traders should not expect markets to react much to the upcoming CPI report. Instead, it will monitor the continued tensions in Iran. Moreover, shipment delays through the Strait of Hormuz will sway markets in the near-term.

data.title: Vermilion Energy: A Deep Value Natural Gas Play That's Still Too Cheap
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Vermilion Energy (VET), I reiterated their Strong Buy rating, highlighting their significant discount to both intrinsic and book value, with solid assets and an improving balance sheet, while the company's US exit and portfolio refinancing supported a potential re-rating.

Despite the stock now being up about 30% since the previous coverage in January and over 50% since the initial rating back in late October, I reiterate VET’s Strong Buy rating, as the company continues to improve and remains on a good trajectory, while the valuation continues to be very attractive.

Internal Developments

VET reported a good Q4 and 2025 overall, beating the market's revenue estimates, delivering record annual production, and exceeding their Q4 production guidance, with very solid improvements in unit costs and CAPEX/flowing boe estimated for 2026 thanks to their portfolio recycling.

This is ultimately something very important that I’m trying to highlight about this company. Sure, nobody is denying that they’ve had bad management in the past, they’ve made mistakes, allocated capital inefficiently, etc., but many of these managers/directors are gone, and we could even call this a massive, top-to-bottom fundamental transformation, changing significantly in recent years, growing reserves per share, cutting costs, and building a strong portfolio with long-life assets.

Despite the high CAPEX and macro pressure, we can see their strategy paying out already, with the free cash flow reaching a solid C$308.74 million (US$226.24 million) in 2025 compared to C$344.77 million in 2024, which is not bad at all for a ~US$1.75 billion market cap after their jump.

VET also confirmed their full-year guidance, expecting C$600 million to C$630 million in CAPEX (similar to 2025), with 67% of that going into Canada and 33% on their international assets, while the production will continue to be heavily focused on natural gas, further supporting this global transition/portfolio repositioning.

Financially, based on VET’s latest report, we can see a solid position, even though the current assets stand below the current liabilities and their cash and cash equivalents dropped, while they reduced the net debt by over C$700 million (targeting more reductions in the future as they aim to reach a net debt to FFO leverage of 1.0x) since the jump earlier last year as a result of the Westbrick acquisition from about a year ago that expanded their Deep Basin exposure, and I’d add that the repayment and balance sheet focus are a good move in this environment.

VET also increased the dividend by 4% recently to US$0.395 per year (C$0.135 per quarter), for a roughly 3.6% yield at the moment of writing this, as well as C$35.75 million (US$26.16 million) in buybacks, for an additional ~1.5% yield. Combined with ~US$60 million in dividends and based on the kind of cash flow VET is able to produce, we're talking about a very sustainable payout ratio, and there is plenty of cash flow left to improve the balance sheet or focus on additional growth or returns.

Macro Developments

To begin with the attack against Iran, we can see a different initial response than expected, followed by Iran’s threat regarding the Strait of Hormuz, which is key for international oil, gas, fertilizers, etc., and the subsequent strikes launched by the country against its neighbors.

Although Trump said the US Navy could escort ships through the strait, this conflict is very important on a global scale and can go well beyond it, as it involves countries that are behind the Petrodollar’s strength and natural gas/LNG giants such as Qatar - which produces roughly 20% of global LNG - with the latter’s recent output halt following Iran’s strikes leading to a ~50% jump in European gas on the first day of trading following it, while fuel prices also rose.

To briefly mention Venezuela as well, I’ll reiterate that I believe Exxon (XOM) summarized it perfectly as “uninvestible,” as even though we’re talking about the biggest reserves on the planet, we’re also looking at one of the heaviest oils (needs a lot of processing) and ancient infrastructure, all on top of political and economic pressure that makes taking this risk extremely hard to justify to shareholders, especially at a time when many players are cutting their CAPEX.

Still, Exxon said a few days ago that they’re going to send a team to Venezuela in a few weeks, although it could still take many years until they're back in the country (they left in 2007).

There are several differences to note here, however, and if the final goal is a regime change (and maintaining it), this situation may last for way more than Trump’s expected few weeks. Again, we’re talking about a natural fortress that has been preparing for something like this for decades, which lacks the kind of organized/armed opposition that the previous (failed) attempts that involved American soldiers on the ground (e.g. Afghanistan, Vietnam - roughly 2 decades each), on top of being more than twice the size of Afghanistan, having four-season weather, mountains, all of which are very important differences from a military point of view, and I don’t think anyone is ready for that.

Sure, this could be just a short-term “push” for negotiations, but every bit of uncertainty and high fuel prices adds more pressure on an already fragile economy, marked by significant consumer weakness as a result of the rate-hike slowdown accentuated by tariffs and other macro uncertainty.

OPEC+ has so far been acting as expected, recently approving a modest boost for April a day after the attack, but they could be a major winner in case commodity prices continue their jump, although potential for higher production hikes could act as a ceiling to how high they can go, as the demand side can also be affected.

Looking at it from a fundamental point of view, we’re still talking about a structural surplus, and if there's no long-term blockade or conflict in the region, there’s a high chance that the fundamental pressures have the final word on the price, as production from many countries is at record highs (especially if high prices persist) despite weak demand (which would also be affected by high prices), even though there’s a chance that near-term prices could even reach $100/bbl, as said very well by Wood Mackenzie analysts recently:

In this conflict, the stakes are higher still with 15 million b/d of Gulf crude and product exports under threat. Failure to quickly re-establish flows through the Strait of Hormuz could again drive prices well over US$100/bbl from Friday’s close under US$73/bbl (Brent).

As for the longer term, LNG’s potential remains very strong thanks to its role as a transition fuel and AI’s massive energy demand that can support significant demand, with studies highlighting potential for very high CAGRs (11.6% from 2025 to 2030) and significant shifts following the war in Ukraine.

Meanwhile, since VET has strong natural gas exposure and doesn’t depend solely on local AECO prices, they can continue to enjoy international prices, especially as US and even Canadian LNG projects come online, potentially lifting local prices as well since the premium wouldn’t have to be so deep.

At the same time, we can see a shift in Canada’s stance on oil and gas following the Grand Bargain MOU, with the country likely aiming at cutting its dependency on the US, and I’ll also mention that the country has significant potential as a top data center location given their cheap resources, weather and potential for regulatory support, with Alberta launching a dedicated “AI Data Centre Strategy” to attract $100 billion in investments about a year ago, while the federal government is also reportedly working on something in this direction, with some proposals announced a few months ago.

Risks Reiterated

Overall, although I remain very bullish on Canadian oil and especially gas and can definitely see a permanent re-rating/re-pricing in their assets going forward, VET will always play in a very volatile market, with significant impacts from politics, wars, economics, speculation, etc., ultimately impacting their valuation and performance.

This is very important to keep in mind, as I see more and more concerns about various oil and gas producers whose stocks are underperforming, even though it’s ultimately normal since they remain tied to the commodity.

Of course, management and past mistakes also play a role in this underperformance, and even though I see more upside for VET, we should keep in mind that their focus on Canada is not guaranteed to deliver. And, even a couple of weeks ago, we’ve just seen Australian regulators shutting down their legacy Wandoo’s exports after an oil spill in December and repeated “systemic failures” in their environmental management systems, so we really shouldn’t ignore this kind of risk.

Valuation

For VET's DCF to Equity valuation, assuming constant commodity prices going forward, I'll go for the same $200 million FCF I expect in 2026 (note that everything in this valuation is converted in USD), thanks to it being a transition year, followed by a jump to $300 million in 2027 thanks to their CAPEX falling, German project entering first production and ramping up soon, growing at a 3.5% CAGR until 2030 thanks to other improvements, interest reductions, capital efficiencies, etc., followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate given the potential of Canada and natural gas, with a 12.5% discount rate to take into account an even better margin of safety given the volatility, as well as 155.18 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Note that I won’t account for net borrowings here, as I want to highlight their pure cash generation potential, and I believe the current level of debt is manageable, especially since their leverage can be reduced through FFO growth instead.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.95 billion, for an intrinsic value of $19.00, which is still well above the current $11.00 in the post-market at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

I reiterate VET’s Strong Buy rating, even though the stock is up significantly since I started covering them, backed by a strong and improving portfolio, solid financials, and very good cash flows.

There are risks to keep in mind, as we’re talking about a commodity company first and foremost, but I remain very bullish on natural gas and Canada overall, and VET remains very undervalued from many points of view.

data.title: SPY Is Now A Tech ETF: Building A Quantitatively Optimized 'Strong Buy' Alternative
data.description:
Introduction

The S&P 500 is currently historically concentrated in the largest companies. In the last ten years, that trend has been a huge tailwind for passive ETF investors, as more of their capital was unwittingly allocated to mega-cap stocks experiencing huge growth. However, with the S&P so heavily concentrated in the top few stocks by market cap, the pendulum may be ready to swing back in the other direction, favoring custom portfolio construction once again.

Seeking Alpha’s “Strong Buy” picks provide investors on the platform a tool to identify high-quality stocks. However, in investing, picking the best stocks is only half the battle. In this article, I aim to leverage Seeking Alpha’s quantitative ratings to create a portfolio consisting of great stock picks that is also quantitatively optimized. I use techniques of Markowitz mean-variance optimization, Monte Carlo simulation, and Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage to create an optimal portfolio of “Strong Buy” picks as of March 1st, 2026.

Stocks as Behavioral Entities

The key insight of modern portfolio theory, as I see it, is that in addition to a stock’s underlying fundamentals, it also has behavioral characteristics. That means that a portfolio can be more than the sum of its parts if it consists of stocks that have complementary behaviors. Similar to how an organization with a myriad of different personality types might function better than one in which every person is a similar type of personality, a portfolio that consists of stocks that have different “personalities” is likely to perform better on a risk-adjusted basis. As Harry Markowitz famously said, “Diversification is the only free lunch in investing.”

Most investors assume that buying an S&P 500 index fund is the best way to achieve diversification. But in 2026, that assumption may be losing its validity. Currently, the top 10 names in the S&P 500 dominate the index, with 41% of the combined market capitalization (a record high). And those top 10 names are typically stocks involved in AI and tech. Out of the top 10, only Berkshire Hathaway is not a tech company. These top ten companies tend to be highly correlated with one another, which is the opposite of what you would want in a diversified portfolio. The chart below shows the degree to which the top 10 has dominated the S&P index over the last 65 years:

So investors buying index funds that track the major indexes like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq may not realize that they’re exposed to significant concentration risk by investing in an index fund. So far, S&P ETFs have done well because the top companies have done so well, but that is not guaranteed to be the case going forward. For someone buying SPY or IVV in 2014, they would have been getting a more diversified portfolio than someone buying those ETFs today is. There are also “equal weight” S&P 500 ETFs, but equal weighting still doesn’t achieve optimal diversification because you might have a relatively high number of similar companies in the index.

So that got me thinking: what if we combine Seeking Alpha’s stock ranking system with quantitative portfolio optimization to select a portfolio that both consists of stocks that have strong fundamentals and that achieves the “free lunch” of optimal diversification? I’m a strong believer in both Buffett-style fundamental analysis and portfolio optimization, so in this project I aim to combine the two to the greatest effect I can.

Methodology

Stock Selection

I used Seeking Alpha’s screening tool to narrow down the universe of stocks for inclusion in this portfolio. I selected US-based stocks with Quant “Strong Buy” ratings with market caps over $4 billion and individual share prices under $5000. That narrowed the list down to 89 stocks. I then pulled adjusted closing prices for these stocks into Python using the yfinance API. I narrowed the data down to prices from January 1, 2017, to today and eliminated 11 stocks from consideration that had missing data (likely companies that were not publicly traded for the entire period). That left 78 stocks to use as a basis for constructing this optimized portfolio. You can download the CSV file I used for this project here: Strong_Buys_List.csv

Modeling Expected Returns and Volatility

One way to model future expected returns and volatilities is to simply use observed average return and observed volatility (historical standard deviation). This approach tends to work well for volatility but less well for actual return. So I used a few statistical techniques to attempt to model expected returns more accurately. First, I winsorized the distribution of daily returns for each stock, which means that I trimmed the extreme outliers from the data (the top 2.5% of daily returns take the value of the 97.5th percentile, and the bottom 2.5% of returns take the value of the 2.5th percentile). That way, huge gains or losses like +/- 30% in one day don’t affect the expected return value. Then, rather than just calculating the average daily return and multiplying by 252 to get an annual return, I simulated a year of trading by randomly selecting 252 daily returns from the winsorized data of daily returns from 2017 to present for each stock. I repeated the simulation 1000 times for each stock and used the median result for each stock as a proxy for its expected return and volatility.

By simulating expected return and expected volatility, we can determine the top and bottom picks based on their “synthetic” Sharpe ratios (using a risk-free rate of 4%). The chart below shows the top 5 and bottom 5 stocks based on simulated Sharpe ratios:

Note the best stocks by synthetic Sharpe ratio may not be the top holdings in the optimized portfolio. For example, Arista Networks (ANET) and Lumentum Holdings (LITE) both have very high Sharpe ratios, but the two stocks are also highly correlated with each other (i.e., they exhibit similar behavioral characteristics). So having a heavy concentration of both stocks in a portfolio might be like trying to play with two quarterbacks. Patrick Mahomes and Lamar Jackson may both be great players, but you wouldn’t want both on the same team.

Calculating the Optimal Portfolio

The first step in optimizing the portfolio is constructing a covariance matrix. I used the Ledoit-Wolf estimator to create the covariance matrix used in the optimization calculation. Studies have found that this method tends to produce better estimates of true covariance than a standard covariance matrix does.

Next, I run a sequential least squares programming algorithm to determine the optimal portfolio weights, with the following constraints:

  • No single holding can make up more than 12% of the overall portfolio, and no single holding can be less than 0% of the portfolio (no short-selling).

  • The sum of all weights must equal 1

All 78 stocks were given some weight in the optimized portfolio, but some weights were so low as to be impractical to actually include. So I narrowed down the portfolio to only the stocks that had a greater than 1% weight in the optimized portfolio, and I ran the optimization algorithm again to get final weightings. This produced a portfolio of 15 stocks. The following graphic shows the weights in the final optimized portfolio and how that portfolio compares in risk-adjusted terms to an equal-weighted portfolio of all 78 Strong Buy stocks:

Interestingly, we get lower volatility from a smaller portfolio compared to a portfolio of 78 equal-weighted stocks. Typically, historical volatility is a good proxy for future volatility, so although I am taking some liberties in forecasting expected returns, the expected volatility of the portfolio is likely to be a decent estimate of actual volatility. I also calculated the average return and volatility of SPY over the same time frame: the annualized return is 14.78%, and the annualized volatility is 18.38%. So SPY, with 500 holdings, seems to be more volatile than the optimized portfolio of 15 holdings, while the optimized portfolio is expected to achieve significantly higher returns (albeit based on historical simulation assumptions). This shows how combining even relatively few complementary assets in the right proportions can perform better in both risk and return than simply owning everything.

The top five holdings show how the algorithm sought diversified selection. IESC (Industrials - Construction/Engineering), LMT (Industrials - Defense & Aerospace), PM (Consumer Staples), AU (Materials - Gold), and ANET (Information Technology) span very diverse sectors/industries. The correlation matrix of the top 8 holdings helps explain why these selections go well together:

AU is a particularly strong pick because of its low correlation with all the other top 8 stocks. ANET, LITE, and IESC have quite high correlation between them, but that intercorrelation is balanced out by low correlations with stocks like LMT, PM, and AU.

Interestingly, the quantitative optimization seemed to gravitate toward what I would call a “barbell approach.” This table shows the stocks in the optimized portfolio, their sector, industry, and approximate beta. You can see that we have a bifurcation between several higher-beta IT stocks (or stocks orbiting the AI/data center cycle) and then lower-beta stocks in Industrials/Materials/Consumer Staples/Healthcare:

The overall beta for the portfolio is 0.96, indicating a similar level of risk to the overall S&P 500.

Backtesting the Methodology

To verify that the methodology actually works, we can pretend we’re at the start of a prior period of time (say 2024) and see what the model would have recommended at that time and then how it would have performed. So we make the model blind to data after January 2024 and see how well it would have worked.

Running the algorithm on pre-2024 data produced a portfolio of 14 stocks. Some of the picks are overlapping - ANET, AU, BOOT, IESC, LMT, DDS, MU - while others are new - AMD, AMZN, BRK.B, DELL, MOD, RUSHA, STLD. The predicted return for the portfolio in 2024 was 27%, with 22% volatility, while actual results were 48.6% return with 24.8% volatility. So both in absolute terms and in risk-adjusted terms, the selected portfolio outperformed the expected metrics.

The major limitation to this backtest is that I don’t have access to Seeking Alpha’s “Strong Buys” at that time, so we’re using today’s “Strong Buy” list as a stand-in. The “Strong Buy” rated stocks would have been different at the start of 2024 than they are now. So that does limit the ability to truly backtest the methodology, since it introduces the possibility of survivorship bias and look-ahead bias. Just looking at some of these tickers, some were on the “Strong Buy” list in January 2024, like ANET and AU, while quite a few were rated “Hold” - AMZN, LMT, and MU for example.

Limitations & Word of Warning

I want to make it very clear that quantitative portfolio optimization hinges heavily on return assumptions for the individual stock picks. To estimate returns for individual stocks, I try to use a more robust methodology than simply using the historical average return. However, I am still using historical data to predict the future, which is an assumption that does a lot of heavy lifting in the analysis.

So that this analysis doesn’t rely too heavily on return assumptions, I also calculated the weights for a minimum variance portfolio of the “Strong Buy” stocks (stripping out any assumptions about expected returns). Here are the weights for that portfolio:

So this portfolio would be optimal for an investor seeking the lowest volatility out of the universe of “Strong Buy” stocks. It is clearly more skewed toward the lower beta names, but there is still substantial overlap with the stocks picked in the risk-return optimized portfolio.

Another thing to consider is that optimizing a portfolio in this way is a bit of a moving target. Ever since I started this analysis, one stock (IESC) has dropped from a Strong Buy to a Hold rating. This seems to be due to a change in the EPS Rev score from A+ to F. As new stocks become Strong Buys and current Strong Buys lose their status, the portfolio optimization will change. So the specific constitution of the portfolio will depend on when we gather data and run the algorithm.

Final Thoughts

What should the takeaway be for investors? This project is meant to be a demonstrative exercise. You don’t necessarily need to imitate this exact portfolio, but I believe the lesson to be learned here is that it may be a good time to construct diversified portfolios of individual stocks (along with other assets potentially) again. For quite a long time the prevailing belief has been that passive investing in ETFs is a superior strategy to active stock picking and portfolio construction. That may have been true when indexes like the S&P 500 were well diversified, but when a few mega-cap stocks make up such a large percentage of the index, then an ETF tracking that index no longer represents optimal diversification in my view. Furthermore, even at other points in history during which the S&P 500 was dominated by the top 10 stocks (1960, 2000), those stocks were not all thematically concentrated. In 2000, the top 10 consisted of stocks like Microsoft, GE, Walmart, Exxon Mobil, and Pfizer. Even though there was heavy concentration in the top 10 at that time, they still spanned different sectors and industries.

So even though passive ETF investing has worked well in recent history, it gathers more risk as more market cap is sucked up into the top IT stocks. Therefore, I assign a “Hold” rating to the various S&P 500 ETFs (SPY, IVV, VOO). In my view, owning these ETFs alone is no longer an optimal investment strategy. I believe that investors can achieve better risk-adjusted returns by owning a diverse basket of stocks or ETFs instead of just owning a single index fund.

If you enjoyed this analysis and would like to see it updated/improved upon later this year, let me know in the comments. If people are interested, I can add a link to view and download the Jupyter Notebook file for this project in the comments as well. Thanks for reading!

data.title: Insteel Industries Is Getting Close To An Upgrade
data.description:
Back in September of last year, I decided to dig back into Insteel Industries (IIIN), an interesting player in the building materials space. For those not familiar with the business, it produces prestressed concrete strand as well as welded wire reinforcements such as ESM. These are used heavily in the construction sector. And considering how large that is in the US, this is essentially a play on the American economy long term. As interesting as the company is, however, I have not been able to pull the trigger on an upgrade for the stock. For quite a while now, I have had it rated a "Hold." And at the time that article was published, I felt that upgrading it was premature.

Since then, the stock has risen 2.2%, falling just short of the 3.7% rise that the S&P 500 has seen. Since I originally downgraded it to a "Hold" in May 2024, the stock has risen 27.1%. This is only slightly worse than the 29.9% increase that the market enjoyed. Considering that a "Hold" candidate in my book is one that should perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future, I would say that my history with the company has been solid. And even though the stock has gotten cheaper in some respects, and even though we are seeing some rapid growth right now, I think that it would be a bit premature still to upgrade it.

I can’t steel my resolve to upgrade this play

Operationally speaking, I would say that Insteel Industries is an interesting prospect. The company has a relatively simple business model. And the end uses for what it sells can only be described as concentrated. About 70% of what the company generates in the way of revenue comes from concrete product manufacturers. And the remaining 30% involves distributors, rebar fabricators, and contractors. Overwhelmingly, the company is focused on the non-residential construction market. About 85% of its revenue comes from that space, with the remaining 15% coming from residential construction.

With this context provided, I would say that the overall financial performance for the business lately has not been shocking. As I have detailed in other articles, the residential construction space is under pressure right now. Years of inflationary pressures, followed by high interest rates aimed at combating them, not to mention other issues like tariffs, have proven harmful for the housing market. But thanks in large part to private industry making investments in things like data centers, as well as federal and state agencies continuing to allocate capital toward public infrastructure projects, the fact that 85% of the company's revenue comes from non-residential construction works well in its favor.

During the most recent quarter, which is the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year, revenue for the business came in at $159.9 million. This was an increase of 23.3% over the $129.7 million the business reported a year earlier. This sales increase was driven heavily by the welded wire reinforcement category of the firm. Overall revenue there jumped from $82.5 million to $108.4 million. That's a rise of 31.4% year over year. By comparison, prestressed concrete strand revenue rose a more modest 9.1% from $47.3 million to $51.6 million.

According to the business, the improvement that the company saw was driven in large part by higher average selling prices. In fact, that added 18.8% to the top line with the company. Management chalked this up to price increases aimed at recovering the escalation and raw material costs that the business was facing. In essence, when the firm has to contend with higher prices, it merely passes those on to consumers. Some of the price increase that the company saw was driven by tight domestic supply conditions. But a big contributor was clearly the Section 232 steel tariffs implemented by the current U.S. administration. To be clear, these tariffs on steel amount to 50%.

The fact of the matter is that the tariff situation both helps and hurts the company. This requires some context. Take wire rod as an example. This serves as the company's primary raw material. Total domestic production is about 3.5 million tons annually. Meanwhile, U.S. production of all hot-rolled carbon steel is about 100 million tons. But in recent years, the industry has seen the permanent closure of two producing mills and issues at a third one that have meaningfully reduced overall output. The result here is that actual domestic production of wire rod has fallen by more than 800,000 tons per year, while the domestic capacity to produce wire rod has fallen about 1.2 million tons per year compared to domestic consumption, which stands at about 5 million tons annually.

Management has calculated, essentially, that almost 25% of apparent domestic consumption is now offline. This has caused the company to look offshore for its raw materials. On the other hand, however, while the raw materials are costing the business more, the fact that it produces essentially all of its prestressed concrete strand, ESM, and concrete reinforcing products gives it a competitive advantage over firms that must produce and then import these products.

Despite this, the business actually did see some benefit from shipments rising as well. They grew 3.8% year over year. Management claimed that this was caused by favorable demand trends when it came to infrastructure and commercial construction markets, in addition to benefits associated with acquisition activities.

Management has been optimistic about non-residential construction thanks to robust infrastructure spending and data center activity. In the company's earnings call for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, management talked about this in more detail. They actually said that they expect strength in the data center market to remain robust into next year. Outside of this, they said that other parts of the private non-residential construction market are weak right now. So they view this as a bridge until such time that other aspects of private industry rebound.

Thanks to higher revenue, profitability for the company improved also. Net income jumped from $1.1 million to $7.6 million. We saw operating cash flow plunge from $19 million to negative $0.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we get a near doubling from $6.9 million to $12.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $6.1 million to $13.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year compared to 2024. This showed continued growth on the top and bottom lines for the business. This is great to see, and to some extent there is evidence that the future for the firm might be even brighter. In the image below, you can see two leading indicators of the non-residential building construction space. The first of these is the Architectural Billings Index, or ABI for short. And the second is the Dodge Momentum Index, or DMI. Both of these indicators tell very different stories. The ABI, for instance, continues to be in a state of contraction.

Seeing as how it is a measure of architecture billings, it looks at demand that is usually out by 12 months or less. When these billings are contracting, it indicates that buildings are likely to be constructed at a slower pace than in the past. On its own, this is a negative for the company. But the DMI has shown something radically different. In recent months, this indicator, which tracks non-residential projects that are entering the planning stage, has skyrocketed. This means that there is a big jump in planning activity, and that usually materializes in the form of increased spending in the space more than a year out. This indicates that we are experiencing a short-term slowdown but that, beyond the next 12 months, strength is likely. This actually makes a lot of sense when you consider continued investments in data centers and the prospect of falling interest rates.

Unfortunately, while management has said that they are optimistic, we don't really have an idea what the rest of this year will look like. I think it would be more appropriate, then, to value the company in relation to financial performance for 2024 and 2025. Doing that, we can see in the chart below how the stock is priced. Honestly, using the 2025 figures, the stock isn't bad. I was even tempted to upgrade it, especially when you consider that it is trading on the cheap compared to other companies in the building materials space. In the chart below, for instance, you can see that on a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the five firms that I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper than it is. One of them was cheaper, and another was tied with it when it came to the price-to-operating cash flow approach. And finally, when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

I came closer to upgrading Insteel Industries today than I have the last couple of times I have written about it. I am especially enthusiastic about the DMI data. Having said that, I am concerned by broader economic conditions. My expectation is that the United States will likely enter into a recession before long. And while some aspects of spending will remain robust, others will weaken in the same way that the residential construction market has. So until we can see some changes on the economic front, or until we get some idea that the strength seen in the most recent quarter is part of a paradigm shift as opposed to a blip on the radar, I believe that maintaining the stock as a "Hold" is prudent.

data.title: Burlington: Stable Growth, Pick Your Spots
data.description:
Burlington Stores, Inc. (BURL) is a retailer that is over 50 years old now. It was, of course, founded in 1972 as the Burlington Coat Factory in New Jersey. Over time it has grown from a small player to regional outerwear wholesaler and now into a major national off-price retailer. The name stems from its opening location and headquarters in Burlington, New Jersey. Today the company operates over 1,000 stores across the United States and Puerto Rico.

It is now just Burlington, as the name was simplified because it has more than just coats and such. It has a big diversified product assortment. It does compete with other off-price retailers, and has had its share of struggles, but shoppers can get up to 60% off compared to traditional retailers. Like competitors the company does opportunistic buying of overstocks and closeouts from manufacturers.

As we look forward, shareholders have a long-term investment case here because the company continues to expand its footprint with plans to reach 2,000 locations. Shares have had a big rally from the fall of 2025 which was a buying opportunity for traders at our investing group, but for now we have to rate it a Hold at these levels.

That said, long term, there is upside, but a better price can likely be had this year. Still, it’s a positive Hold, and the just-reported earnings justify hanging on for longer-term upside. Let us discuss.

Burlington Q4 Sales And Margins

First, we have to check for trends in sales. That is key. In Q4, which covers the holiday sales period mind you we saw revenues up 11.3% to $3.64 billion. This was a slight beat of $50 million versus consensus, but stable and respectable growth billion. Of course for retailers the most important metric we monitor is always comparable sales. Well, comparable store sales were up 4% from a year ago. For all of 2025 the comps were still positive at 2%. The company had taken action to mitigate tariffs in H1 2025, and that translated in H2 2025.

Now, we love to see higher sales and positive comps. But we also like to see expanding gross profit margin, or at the very least, flat margins. Gross profit margins on a percentage basis rose 80 basis points to 43.7%, from 42.9% for the Q4 compared to last year. We view this as a very positive result. Both freight costs and merchandise margins improved, by 20 basis points and 80 basis points, respectively. So, higher sales, better margins.

The next thing to look for in retail is selling and admin expenses. In total dollars these were up to $1.05 billion vs. $0.965 million last year. But here is the key. As a percentage of sales the selling and admin expenses improved 40 basis points to 22.2% compared to 22.6% last year. Accounting for the commensurate rise, but well-controlled operating expenses, adjusted net income was $320 million, or $4.99 per share, vs. $267 million, or $4.14 per share. Further the adjusted EBITDA was $562 million, compared to $456 million. All of this implied consumers are responding well to the discounted nature of Burlington's offerings, and driving earnings higher, which is why shares have rallied.

Burlington Inventory Levels And Balance Sheet

In terms of inventory the company bought a lot of overstock to build up for future promotions and sales, so despite the great sales merchandise inventories were up to $1.31 billion to end the year vs. $1.25 million coming into the year a 5% increase. At the store level inventories increased 12%. Reserve inventory, the items that are going to be sent to stores in the coming months, was 40% of total inventory to end the year, down slightly from 46% to start the year. How about the balance sheet?

We like what we are seeing on the balance sheet front too. Liquidity is robust, as is the cash position. To end 2025, Burlington had $2.16 billion in liquidity. Making up the liquidity is $1.23 billion in cash and $926 million in available credit on its revolving ABL facility. Given the inventory levels, there is not a need to spend this cash, but the company has a ton of flexibility with this liquidity should opportunity arise. As the company is embarking to expand, there is a manageable $2.08 billion in total debt, including $1.72 billion in its term loan facility, but no borrowings on the ABL facility. This is not a concerning leverage given the cash and cash flow.

Further, the company is repurchasing shares. In Q4, the company repurchased 223,863 shares for $59 million. More repurchases are in store as there is $385 million remaining on its share repurchase program authorization through May 2027.

Looking Ahead For Burlington

We are long-term bullish, but after the run we think a Hold rating is appropriate at this valuation, even with the respectable growth outlook. Tariffs are still a concern, the company wants to open more stores, and the economy while still robust is on watch. But a good year of sales growth is expected, as for fiscal 2026 management projects revenues increasing 8% to 10%. Further, it is guiding for 1-3% comparable sales.

So why is the actionable suggestion right now to hold, even though we see longer-term upside? Simply market dynamics. We think it best to wait for some market weakness for new money to enter at this valuation. Certainly the expectation from management for $10.95 to $11.45 in EPS for the year is positive. This would be $11.20 at the midpoint, and this is 10.1% growth at the midpoint. However, at $322 at the time of this writing, shares are at a premium for this growth at nearly 29X FWD EPS. Thus, we think a Hold is justified, with new money best served holding out for sub-$300, closer to 25X, if not cheaper.

Strategy overview

The Fund combines an actively-managed quantitative equity investment strategy with a call writing option strategy, seeking to create a diversified portfolio with enhanced total return potential and strong downside capture over a full market cycle.

Key takeaways

  • During the quarter, the equity sleeve underperformed the reference Index primarily due to the lower beta positioning and higher dividend yield. Conversely, the core model, particularly the sentiment pillar contributed the most.
  • Global conditions in 4Q25 shifted toward monetary easing as inflation moderated and labor markets softened, prompting the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut rates. Tariff pressures persisted, but cautious consumer spending helped prevent inflation from sticking. U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth slowed yet remained positive, favoring large-cap equities supported by strong fundamentals and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven productivity.
  • Looking ahead to early 2026, global equities remain uneven. Europe and the United Kingdom face fiscal and structural challenges, while Japan benefits from reforms. China struggles with property stress and weak consumer confidence, though tech-linked offshore equities offer opportunities. Emerging markets (EM) are mixed, and international bonds remain less attractive.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund provided a total return of 1.07% on a net asset value basis and a total return of 0.57% on a market price basis. For the same period, the Fund's reference Index, the MSCI World Value Index, returned 3.34%

On the regional level, stock selection in United Kingdom, Japan, and Europe – Euro regions detracted., and selection in the Europe – Non-Euro contributed the most.

data.title: Flywire Corporation: Visible Path For Durable Adj. EBITDA Growth
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Flywire Corporation (FLYW) was a buy rating because all the growth metrics were healthy, and I believed there were tangible growth catalysts previously. Fast forward ~18 months later, growth is still solid, and FLYW is pushing deeper into workflows through SFS. Margins were messy this quarter, but EBITDA still grew strongly, which supports the operating leverage angle.

4Q25 Results Update

FLYW continues to show growth strength. In this Q4 quarter, reported revenue was up 34% y/y to $157.5 million, albeit Sertifi added $14.2 million, or 1200 bps of growth. Without that, organic growth was still >20%, which is very healthy. Viewed from a revenue-less ancillary services basis (revenue excluding pass-through printing/mailing and marketing fees), revenue was $152.7 million, which was up 35.3% y/y. Operating metrics also saw good trends: total payment volume [TPV] was up ~36% y/y to $9.3 billion, and 31% when excluding Sertifi. The part that made this quarter not as clean was margins. GAAP gross margin contracted close to 600 bps y/y (now at 57.6%), and adj. gross margin contracted by close to 470 bps y/y. Management did say the contraction was due to the mix and ramping up of certain payment processing programs, plus currency settlement impacts. That said, adj. EBITDA still grew strongly by ~52% y/y. Which means adj. EBITDA could’ve been stronger if not for these headwinds.

The Growth Profile Becomes More Durable, and the Business Gets Stickier

While growth is not as strong as I thought previously, I think what matters today is that FLYW showed the market it can grow without needing a perfect education admission backdrop. And understanding how FLYW does it instills confidence that this growth is durable. It is essentially what FLYW calls "Enterprise Transformation via SFS," which is FLYW’s effort to embed itself into how schools run billing and collections, using Student Financial Software [SFS] as the pivot. This changes the growth dynamic entirely because growth is no longer tied to the number of student admissions but rather to being part of a workflow. That makes FLYW a lot harder to displace, and it should make growth less dependent on intake season. And the data points supporting this are pretty direct. Management noted >35% growth in 2025 projected annual recurring revenue [ARR] from new signings (excluding healthcare payment processing contracts) and also a 35% y/y increase in new sales pipeline generation. They also said 80% of education revenue and 70% of travel revenue now come from enterprise clients (those with more than $100,000 of LTM revenue).

This workflow push also shows up in how FLYW is building the “plugs” around SFS and payments. This seems to be an area that some investors may have underestimated. Specifically, FLYW noted they are “Workday Certified” for both Global Payments and SFS and also have an Oracle Fusion integration aimed at UK education. They have also expanded payment rails, including stablecoin capabilities and an expanded TenPay partnership that integrates WeChat Pay for Chinese students paying in South Korea and Malaysia. The reason these efforts are important, in my view, is simple. It doesn’t make sense for schools to adopt another point solution that creates extra steps for their finance teams, and students would find it a hassle (and less trustworthy) when they’re trying to make payment. If FLYW can be plugged into systems these schools already run on and support the payment methods students actually use, I believe it is logical to assume schools will adopt FLYW. The implication is it makes winning deals easier, and it increases the odds that once FLYW is in, it doesn’t get replaced.

Valuation

One last positive aspect to note is operating leverage. As noted in the earnings review section, adj. gross margin fell y/y, but adj. EBITDA still went up by more than 50% to $25.4 million (a 16.6% margin), and GAAP net income went to basically break-even from a loss last year. By my math, FLYW added ~$9 million adj. EBITDA on ~$40 million more revenue (vs. Q4 2024), which means an incremental margin of 22.5%. That is the baseline to think about how much more margin can expand. If we extrapolate this forward and believe management's mid-teens revenue growth guide (assuming no acceleration), I can bridge to this business printing ~20% adj. EBITDA growth CAGR through FY28. A business of this growth profile shouldn’t trade at 7x forward EBITDA if a peer like Bill Holdings (BILL) trades at 12x with mid-teens revenue growth. I think FLYW should at least trade closer to 12x forward EBITDA (now trades at ~7x forward EBITDA vs. ~18x in Sep'24).

Risk

My biggest worry is the market view of FLYW’s profitability. The decline in adj. gross margin is huge, and while I focus on adj. EBITDA growth: if more growth comes from lower-margin payment flows (including the mix shifts management noted), margin could compress further, and the operating leverage angle I mentioned may not play out. That would be a disaster for the stock as both sales and earnings growth decelerate (margin could turn back to negative). Execution of the “Enterprise Transformation via SFS” strategy also carries risk. If integrations and rollouts take longer than expected, it could hurt the pace of growth.

Conclusion

My view for FLYW is a buy rating. The market seems to focus too much on the margin noise and ignores the fact that FLYW can continue to print solid growth and that initiatives being pushed are making the business stickier over time. I also like that operating leverage showed up even with gross margin pressure. The main thing to watch is whether the mix keeps dragging gross margin and caps profit expansion. If that stabilizes, the stock should see a strong re-rating as the market prices in solid adj. EBITDA growth ahead.

data.title: BNY Mellon Small/Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Heading into 2026, the macro backdrop reflects a measured balance.

Equity performance was broadly positive across developed and emerging markets. Cooling inflation across major economies, resilient economic growth and improving visibility on corporate earnings supported investor confidence, despite ongoing geopolitical and policy uncertainty. Risk sentiment was further boosted by selective central bank easing and year-end portfolio rebalancing, underpinning gains across global equities.

The S&P 500® Index increased by 2.66% for the quarter. Within the index, eight of the 11 Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) equity sectors finished in positive territory, led by health care and communication services. Value stocks outperformed growth stocks. Among other major equity benchmarks, the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of developed markets excluding the US and Canada, increased by 4.86%, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index increased by 4.73%.

In the US, equities generally advanced during the fourth quarter. The Dow Jones Industrial Average® increased by 4.03%, while the NASDAQ 100 Index® increased by 2.47%. US fixed-income markets also posted gains. Markets proved resilient amid elevated uncertainty driven by a prolonged federal government shutdown and ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Inflation, as measured by the headline consumer-price index, eased gradually from 3.0% year-over-year in September to 2.7% year-over-year in November. Labor market data, while disrupted and delayed, pointed to slowing momentum rather than a sharp deterioration. Economic growth remained firm, with third-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) rising at a solid 4.3% annualized, supported by consumer spending and government outlays despite softer retail sales and business investment. Against this backdrop, the Federal Reserve cut rates by 25 basis points (bps) early in the quarter, while maintaining a cautious tone and reaffirming a data-dependent policy stance as markets looked ahead to 2026.

Equity markets in developed economies were broadly higher during the quarter. In the eurozone, fourth-quarter data signaled easing inflation and mixed growth dynamics. Inflation moderated toward the European Central Bank’s (ECB’s) 2% target, while business activity improved and GDP grew modestly, enabling the ECB to keep rates unchanged throughout the quarter. UK inflation declined steadily from elevated levels, while growth weakened and labor markets softened. The Bank of England shifted from holding rates earlier in the quarter to a 25-bp cut at its December meeting. In Japan, inflation remained above target and unemployment stayed low, despite a third-quarter GDP contraction and softer activity indicators. As a result, the Bank of Japan ended its hold with a late-quarter 25-bp hike, continuing its normalization effort.

Emerging-market equities were positive for the quarter. In China, growth slowed from earlier in the year, and consumer and property-sector weakness persisted; however, data showed tentative stabilization, as manufacturing and services activity returned to slight expansion, enabling the People’s Bank of China to keep lending rates unchanged throughout the quarter. Brazil’s economy softened into the fourth quarter amid widening external deficits and weaker activity indicators. Nonetheless, the central bank maintained its restrictive 15% policy rate despite easing inflation. India stood out with solid momentum, posting robust GDP growth, rising industrial output and relatively contained inflation, supported by government commitments to greater capital spending and reform. In contrast, Mexico faced weak growth and a third-quarter contraction, prompting successive rate cuts as inflation eased. Argentina bolstered financial stability through a sizeable currency-swap agreement and improving US export prospects.

Commodities were slightly higher, with the Refinitiv/CoreCommodity CRB Index rising 0.35%. During the fourth quarter, precious metals posted solid gains, with gold, silver and platinum supported by safe-haven demand amid geopolitical tensions, a US government shutdown and a weaker US dollar, culminating in a robust year-end rally. In contrast, energy markets were softer. Oil prices trended lower throughout the quarter as ample global supply, rising inventories and slowing demand outweighed geopolitical risks. Natural gas was volatile, rising early in the quarter on colder weather, liquefied natural gas export demand and seasonal heating needs, before ending the year lower as weather-driven strength faded. The US dollar weakened against a basket of major currencies.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV)(DBMAX) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, during the fourth quarter of 2025. From a sector perspective, health care led relative contributors over the period, followed by information technology. Conversely, industrials and financials were the most significant laggards.

Sector Review

Positive Impacts

Health Care: Health care led relative contributors over the period. Positive stock selection in the health care providers and services and life sciences tools and services segments contributed.

Information Technology: The information technology sector also contributed positively to relative performance. Solid stock selection within the semiconductors and semiconductor equipment segment was a key driver, supported by effective positioning and favorable stock selection within the technology hardware, storage, and peripherals subsector.

Negative Impacts

Industrials: Stock selection in industrials weighed on relative performance, particularly within the aerospace and defense and construction and engineering segments.

Financials: Financials also detracted from relative returns over the quarter, largely due to positioning and stock selection within banks and insurance companies.

Stock Review1

Key Contributors – Q4 25

Guardant Health: Guardant Health (GH) continued to benefit from its upbeat third-quarter earnings release, news that the company had boosted its blood-testing capabilities and analyst upgrades.

Repligen Corporation: We continue to favor Repligen’s (RGEN) exposure to secularly growing bioprocessing capabilities. Its share price was boosted by the release of better-than-expected third-quarter results that revealed company-wide revenue growth.

Cidara Therapeutics: Shares of Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) surged after the company announced in November that it would be acquired by Merck.

Key Detractors – Q4 25

AeroVironment: AeroVironment designs, develops, produces, delivers, and supports a technologically advanced portfolio of intelligent, multi-domain robotic systems and related services for government agencies and businesses. Shares declined after the company reported disappointing earnings and issued a softer profit outlook.

Inspire Medical Systems: Shares of Inspire Medical Systems fell in the fourth quarter after the company cut its 2025 outlook amid US launch setbacks for the next-generation Inspire V system, while rising competition from GLP‑ 1 weight-loss drugs and shifting patient preference toward drug therapy over surgery further pressured the stock.

Soleno Therapeutics: Shares of Soleno Therapeutics fell in the fourth quarter. A sharp quarter-over-quarter decline in new patient start forms for VYKAT XR and persistent concerns over safety and discontinuation rates overshadowed revenues that exceeded analyst expectations.

1. Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

Market Outlook

Heading into 2026, the macro backdrop reflects a measured balance. Inflation continues to moderate, with the US consumer-price index (CPI) up 2.7% year-over-year and core CPI up 2.6% year-over-year in November, while wage growth and consumer spending have cooled without pointing to recession. In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered its target range to 3.50% – 3.75%, signaling a shift toward easing while reaffirming a data-dependent approach amid mixed committee views. Global growth forecasts hover around 3.2% for 2025 and 3.1% for 2026. Supportive factors include selective fiscal measures in China and a gradual recovery in Europe, while trade policy uncertainty and geopolitical risks remain notable headwinds.

Corporate earnings remain resilient, with S&P 500 earnings per share up nearly 13% in the third quarter and projected to rise 8% in the fourth quarter, underpinned by solid margins and improving breadth beyond mega-cap technology. However, forward price-to-earnings ratios are near 21.8, elevated versus historical averages, suggesting limited capacity for multiple expansion without additional rate relief or better-than-expected earnings. In our view, international equities, particularly developed-market value segments, offer relative attractiveness with lower multiples and potential higher dividend yields, while thematic opportunities in artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure, electrification and energy transition continue to draw capital.

Looking ahead, equities appear biased toward modest gains rather than outsized rallies. We believe positioning may favor quality growth with durable cash flows, complemented by selective cyclicals linked to tangible AI infrastructure and quality small caps that could benefit from policy normalization. Risks include sticky services inflation, labor market deterioration and renewed supply-chain disruptions, while catalysts, such as January consumer-price inflation, Fed communications and fourth-quarter earnings could shape sentiment. In short, we think the environment supports a disciplined allocation framework that balances the potential upside from policy easing and earnings resilience against valuation constraints and geopolitical volatility.

Small-cap equities may be increasingly attractive in 2026, supported by a convergence of macro, policy and earnings momentum. First, anticipated Fed rate cuts in 2026 could benefit small caps, which tend to be more sensitive to lower borrowing costs. Second, valuations remain discounted; Bank of America projects S&P SmallCap 600 earnings-per-share growth of approximately 19% for 2026, above the broader S&P 500, suggesting favorable entry points. Third, cyclical tailwinds like reshoring, AI-driven capital investment, rising merger-and-acquisition activity and signs of a prospective initial-public-offering rebound may offer additional earnings catalysts. We believe that small-cap leadership will likely extend into 2026 as funds rotate out of overvalued mega caps into this historically overlooked segment. Overall, the combination of improving macroeconomics, lower valuations and accelerating earnings momentum suggests a supportive setup for US small caps, with scope for continued recovery and the potential for relative outperformance.

The 2026 outlook for international markets is supported by attractive valuations, policy accommodation in select regions and strengthening macro data. The European Central Bank’s neutral-to-accommodative stance, coupled with fiscal initiatives that are boosting credit growth and industrial activity, supports consensus expectations for approximately 15% earnings-per-share growth in 2026. Meanwhile, Asia may benefit from robust export demand, AI-driven semiconductor momentum and targeted stimulus in China to stabilize property and manufacturing sectors. Regional growth forecasts range from 3.4% to 4.6%. 1 Both regions may offer compelling opportunities in cyclicals, financials and infrastructure-linked sectors, with potential dividend yields and valuation discounts versus US equities adding support. Together, Europe and Asia offer diversified exposure amid a rotation toward broader leadership and structural growth themes.
