data.title: Gen Z Is Threatening The Alcohol Industry
data.description:
Imagine for a moment a scenario where a billion-dollar market realizes its future consumers will no longer buy their product. This might be happening with the alcohol industry. Generation Z is drinking less and less each day, and sometimes, they don’t even start. I’m a millennial who has this trait of Gen Z, since I don’t drink any alcohol at all, not socially, not anything. I might taste something here and there, but nothing more than a sip.

If for millennials this habit sounds strange, imagine for baby boomers… The point is, when an entire generation stops drinking, the impact is not only on pubs and parties but also on public health, taxes, employment, and, of course, global brands and the stock market.

My view here is that I might actually be a bit bearish on any case that treats alcohol as an ever-growing industry. I, personally, wouldn’t buy and hold any beer stock expecting it to grow just as they did in the past, unless the fundamentals of a specific stock are too good and the valuation is extremely attractive. But then it would be more of a math/numbers game than believing in the product or industry.

I’m not saying alcohol will disappear (I’m not that extreme), but I think this “default” social habit is going to change drastically, and the sector will feel it in their sales volume. Therefore, the alcohol industry might have to adapt to bring relevant innovation instead of just producing the same old beers and relying on generational “bad” habits. If that switch to innovation indeed happens, I don’t think all players will be able to afford this R&D, which will make them eventually disappear.

That also doesn’t mean the industry will collapse. What I see is a re-rating of the expectations the market will put on the beer stocks. However, companies with strong branding and pricing power might be able to change their mix and generate sales from low- or no-alcohol beverages and thus remain resilient and profitable.

All in all, I don’t think this is a short-term fluctuation but a behavioral transition. For the investor, this means more scrutiny when stock picking, possibly aiming at companies that have a clear budget/plan for adaptability and innovation rather than mass production only.

Generations and Their Relation With Alcohol

Society puts generations into different boxes because they normally have similar habits, being molded by the same economic, cultural, and technological environment.

If we start with the baby boomers, those are the ones born after World War II. Which means people who grew up in a much more predictable world, with longer professional careers, higher stability, and a habit of drinking alcohol as if it were their second nature, almost like a social ritual.

After the boomers, we have Generation X, which experienced urbanization, higher work intensity, individualism, and the beginning of general market consumption as a lifestyle. This generation started to adopt the “every weekend, I drink” habit.

After Generation X, we have the millennials. Those were born in the midst of an economic crisis, high inflation in multiple parts of the globe, swift changes in the job market, and a clearly more expensive adult life. This generation started to treat alcohol as a personal choice rather than something that was embedded in human nature. But still, most millennials drink at least socially.

Lastly, we get to Generation Z, the first generation that grew up with the internet in the palm of their hands, being constantly influenced by social media and with real-time information at their disposal. With all that load of information and habits being shown to the world, this generation started to realize that they might not need alcohol to have fun or for it to be present in every part of their lives… or maybe not in any part of it. And that’s where the alcohol logic started to fall apart.

Alcohol Consumption Dropped Worldwide

To begin, research shows that Generation Z consumes about 20% less alcohol than millennials. Looking specifically at wine and beer, the gap is even bigger, reaching something like 33% less than previous generations.

Alcohol was present everywhere, from college and happy hours to weddings and birthdays, not necessarily because everyone wanted to drink, but because it was the social norm. Now, pubs and clubs are already finding it hard to attract the new generation, and when they do, how are they going to monetize the clientele without selling alcoholic beverages?

In the beginning, it might seem like a temporary trend, but I believe it’s not. Businesses in those industries now have to change their marketing strategy considerably, and they have to face the fact that they are going to lose all the billions they spent throughout the decades trying to make alcohol look like an essential part of life... and Gen Z already knows it’s not.

Statistics also show that 70% of Gen Z is choosing non-alcoholic beverages, while 18%-20% of young Americans between 21 and 28 years old also don’t drink beer, wine, or spirits on a regular basis. And this is not just in the US. In the UK, for example, one in four young people are abstinent, according to data from the Health Survey for England, and young people aged between 16 and 24 who don’t drink alcohol range from 25% to 30%.

In Germany, alcohol consumption has also dropped. In 2024, 68% of Germans said they drink alcohol, compared to 78% in 2015, according to data from YouGov. Within the German Gen Z, 61% say they drink, but one in four reduced consumption or stopped completely in the past five years. The result? 52 breweries closed between 2023 and 2024, the biggest decline in three decades. Yes, even Germany is taking a hit from the new healthy generation.

Now, talking about my home, Brazil also followed the trend. In 2025, 64% of Brazilians affirmed they didn’t consume alcohol, against 55% in 2023, according to this data from CISA. But then again, the young folks are the ones changing their habits the most. Abstinence rose from 46% to 64% among people between 18 and 24 years old and from 47% to 61% for folks aged between 25 and 34.

All right, enough data spilling. The point is not whether this trend change exists, because it does. The question is how it's affecting the industry, but this time let's look in more detail.

What's the Impact on the Industry

From what I see, when an entire generation consumes less alcohol, three different components feel the major hit: revenue, inventory, and jobs.

Decline in Sales Volume and Market Value

Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), or simply AB InBev, the largest brewer in the world, has already reported a drop in volume. This, in my view, is quite relevant because the company might adjust the price and even the product mix, but volume is linked directly to consumer habits.

In Brazil, volume fell 4.5% in the year and 2.6% in the fourth quarter. In Argentina, it declined 0.9% in the year and 4.9% in the fourth quarter. And in Canada, it fell 1.3% in the year and 0.7% in the quarter. Which means it’s not a local event, as we had already discussed.

Broadly speaking, according to data from IWSR (International Wine and Spirits Record), the total volume for alcoholic beverages dropped 1% in 2024 in major markets, and a new drop of 0.4% is expected for 2025.

The consequence, as mentioned in the beginning, is felt in the financial market as well. The global beverage sector is 46% below its 2021 peak, with a cumulative loss of about $830 billion in market value in just over four years.

High Inventories and Job Cuts

Inventories have also become a major problem, with big and publicly traded spirits companies sitting on a pile of unsold beverages worth about $22 billion. The reason is that this specific market is a big fan of planning things far in advance.

Take the whiskeys, for example, that require more than a decade of aging before being launched in the market. You can’t simply predict with precision that you won’t have consumers decades ahead, so you can’t simply halt your current production.

The outcome? Immobilized capital, storage costs, financial costs, and devaluation risk, which tend to create pressure on margins and a possible price war, as heavy discounts and sales are required.

And finally, this pressure ends up hitting the labor market. Take Heineken N.V. (HEINY), for instance, which announced the cut of 6,000 jobs, about 7% of its global workforce. They explained it was due to a weaker demand and slower expected growth.

The Growth of Non-Alcoholic Beer

As an attempt to adapt, the industry is obviously changing its portfolio, with money migrating from alcoholic beverages to non-alcoholic drinks. In Brazil, for example, non-alcoholic beer consumption jumped from 133 million liters in 2018 to 750 million liters in 2022. On top of that, Euromonitor projects 885 million liters in 2026, almost six times the 2018 volume.

According to IWST, global sales of low- to non-alcoholic drinks reached $11 billion in 2022, a 37.5% increase compared with 2018. This means that the industry was already predicting or experiencing this trend change at some point, repositioning its portfolio. However, the costs for this innovation are high, and not all companies have the capital to invest in R&D of new flavors, marketing strategies, distribution, and product development.

How Investors Can Position for This Trend Change

If what I discussed until now makes sense, especially regarding lower volume growth and pressure on margins and valuation, then there might be some moves investors can make.

Let me take a quick example to better illustrate this dynamic. For instance, Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) Jack Daniel’s owner reported ~$1 billion in quarterly revenue (-5.39% YoY) and signaled a decline in organic sales and profits for the next fiscal year. So, with roughly $4 billion in annual sales, even a modest 5% revenue decline represents a relevant amount of about $200 million in lost sales (0.05 × $4 billion). In the event that Brown-Forman manages to cut $70–$80 million in costs, that would cover only 35–40% of the impact, still leaving $120–$130 million in operating pressure.

Therefore, a possible approach would be to reduce exposure to companies highly dependent on alcohol volume, and then allocate part of this exposure to beverage sectors that are more resilient, if you still wish to stay invested in the industry.

An example is the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP), which is around 19% exposed to beverages, with relevant positions in Coca-Cola (KO) (~6.4%), PepsiCo (PEP) (~4.7%), and Monster (MNST) (~3.6%). So, a $10,000 investment in this ETF would represent about $1,900 of indirect exposure to the beverages sector, but with much more diversification.

Another more “targeted” option would be the Invesco Food & Beverage ETF (PBJ), which has a higher allocation to Monster (~5%) and also includes names like Celsius (CELH) (~3.37%)

Lastly, if you really want to stock pick, I would recommend global brewers with a strong emerging market presence and an inclination to scale their no/low-alcohol products. For this scenario, I can only think of Heineken, which is having tremendous success with its Heineken 0.0 and seems to be engaged and willing to innovate.

So, in short, I believe investors who wish exposure to this sector should approach it as less “betting against alcohol” and more “let’s reallocate within the beverages sector.” And they can do that through those ETFs I mentioned or through companies that clearly intend to do something about this major change in alcohol consumption.

Alcohol Consumption Will Not Disappear

I don’t think at all that alcohol consumption will disappear one day, not even in the distant future. It will simply decline drastically, and the market will have to adapt. As for my personal take on this, I might be biased because I don’t drink, but I can’t deny that I see this as something positive; after all, it’s been proven that alcohol, in any form or shape, is bad for your health.

But as for the financial market and what investors can expect, I would definitely think twice before jumping into any stock or ETF from the beer industry with a buy-and-hold approach. From what we discussed, I don’t have much faith in this general market, but that does not mean you won’t find good bargains here and there. Finally, if you plan on finding asymmetries in this market, go for value instead of growth.
